text: The surgeon creates the preperitoneal space and inserts two 5mm trocars. The epigastric vessels are identified as anatomical landmarks, appearing detached from the abdominal wall, indicating altered anatomy in the region.

The surgeon delicately dissects and liberates the hernia sac, revealing the bladder, which is not covered by the peritoneal sac, indicating its extraperitoneal classification. The bladder is reduced through the hernia orifice, which is lateral to the epigastric vessels, defining it as an external oblique hernia. As the bladder is reduced, funicular structures become visible, and another hernia sac is identified and carefully isolated until reduced.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical area, sectioning the remaining adhesions around the bladder and spermatic cord. A macroporous partially absorbable lightweight mesh is inserted into the extraperitoneal space and secured to Cooper's ligament using absorbable tags.
